title: Changed Perspectives
wikitext:
[]: Changed Perspectives             Quote Post by schismatics » Sat Jul 25, 2009 8:45 am (I wasn't sure if this should've been in discussion of chit-chat so I put it here) So in NGE are there any lines or scenes that changed what you think on life? I mean not like "OMG that scene was AWESOME!!111!" but more like something that made you stop and think and eventually change your ideas on things. For me there were 2 major points. 1 - In episode 16 during the Lelial mindfukc sequence, there was the dialouge that goes on about "the Ikari Shinji in so and so's mind". That whole scene made me think of how a person can appear to someone and yet still have a totally different personality. I know this doesn't sound like much of a revelation of the idea that there's a "you" that exists in someone else's mind was pretty radical the first time I heard it. 2 - My other revelation would be all the talk about freedom. Kaworu in episode 24 (his only episode DUH) says something along the lines of "death may be the only true freedom that there is" (I only remember the Eng. dub's line so I'm not sure if that was the actual line). And there's also episode 26 where a badly drawn Shinji is floating around and there's some talking about "You now have a down. A part of your freedom has been taken away." (not exact but close enough) This had a BIG effect on me and I'm now under the impression that death may actually be the only true freedom. I mean in life you not only have the laws of gravity and such but you also have all the laws of civilization. That is all. What's your opinion??

[]: Re: Changed Perspectives             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Jul 25, 2009 9:14 am schismatics wrote: I'm now under the impression that death may actually be the only true freedom.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Jul 25, 2009 9:59 am Episode 26 was a very enlightening one for me as well. And Asuka's mind rape scene in episode 22 expounds one the idea of one's self as seen by others in episode 16. I don't think Asuka meant to be seen as all of those things that she's seen as in the series, which is why it bothered her when she was forced to confront it. To expound on the idea of freedom : Yes, I am restricted by an up and down. But I can move around easier this way, so now I don't have to think about it, giving my mind freedom to think about other things. (I think that was the logic in episode 26 as well.) "True" freedom is like someone owning his own business. First, he has the freedom to do everything by himself. But that "true" freedom places restrictions on how well he performs those tasks. So he hires others to help do the work for him. Now he's restricted to only the tasks that he didn't delegate to others, but he has more freedom to perform his own tasks better. A car is also often seen as freedom, despite the fact that it restricts the movement of your legs, limits the functions of your hands, and forces you to pay attention to driving only. I don't think anyone really longs for true freedom. We enjoy some of our restrictions too much, and enjoyment is one of the freedoms we have.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat Jul 25, 2009 10:25 am Sure, true freedom should include the freedom to take on restrictions voluntarily... and to throw them right back off again if you change your mind. To address the topic though, Eva has had 2 influences on me. First, it's set the bar by which I judge all other anime. Secondly, the charecters have become almost archetypes in my mind such that when I try to get a grip on a charecter's (or person's) overall feel I tend to mentally consider them "like Asuka except..." or something similar until I get to know them better.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Jul 25, 2009 10:56 am I guess I should address the topic, as well. It's difficult to articulate Evangelion 's effect on me. I suppose the best way I can put it is that it took on the role of a narrative philosophical framework (I could probably use a better phrase) . Not so much in the sense that it provides principles to live by -- although it does offer some of those -- but it's a story about people, how we relate to one another and ourselves, and the daily struggle of living. It all resounded with me deeply, and I internalized it, and it became a part of me. So where some people might use, say, Bible stories as paradigms for episodes (etc.) in their own lives, Evangelion might be serving me instead. I'll put more down if I can wrap it up in a nicer package.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sat Jul 25, 2009 12:22 pm Reichu wrote: Not so much in the sense that it provides principles to live by -- although it does offer some of those -- but it's a story about people, how we relate to one another and ourselves, and the daily struggle of living. It all resounded with me deeply, and I internalized it, and it became a part of me.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Sat Jul 25, 2009 3:10 pm I love the line about death being true freedom, but I'm fairly sure it's a mistranslation. I remember the subtitled version was worded slightly different, but it completelty changed the meaning. It was "To die of one's own will, that is true freedom"or something like that. Which implies that DEATH isn't freedom, but to make the choice of how you will die is freedom. Doesn't resonate as much. Anyway, ditto on the Leliel line, that one gnawed at me for days afterwards. Also, Gendo's line in Ep. 8 about man being man's greatest enemy are words to live by.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Jul 25, 2009 3:14 pm Episode 24 wrote: KAWORU: Because it's my destiny to continue to live even if it may result in the destruction of humanity. But I can also die here. Life and death are of equal value to me. Dying of your own will. That is the one and only absolute freedom there is.

[]: Re: Changed Perspectives             Quote Post by Ornette » Sat Jul 25, 2009 3:18 pm schismatics wrote: So in NGE are there any lines or scenes that changed what you think on life? I mean not like "OMG that scene was AWESOME!!111!" but more like something that made you stop and think and eventually change your ideas on things.

[]:             Quote Post by TheWaffleCult » Sat Jul 25, 2009 3:35 pm Since I'm an animation student myself, the scene where Gendo draws a line to "restrict" Shinji was extremely powerful to me. It was such a simple movement, but the message resonated pretty deeply for me.

[]:             Quote Post by mrkitano » Sat Jul 25, 2009 3:46 pm Since I'm an animation student myself, the scene where Gendo draws a line to "restrict" Shinji was extremely powerful to me. It was such a simple movement, but the message resonated pretty deeply for me.

[]:             Quote Post by TheWaffleCult » Sat Jul 25, 2009 3:56 pm mrkitano wrote: Since I'm an animation student myself, the scene where Gendo draws a line to "restrict" Shinji was extremely powerful to me. It was such a simple movement, but the message resonated pretty deeply for me.

[]:             Quote Post by mrkitano » Sat Jul 25, 2009 4:01 pm Really? You mean the animation technique used in that? That's awesome.

[]:             Quote Post by TheWaffleCult » Sat Jul 25, 2009 4:53 pm Yeah, at the moment I'm doing projects frame by frame on stock paper with pencil to improve my steadiness and such. It's brutal, but like I said, this scene was inspiring in multiple ways. I'd never really thought of it that way.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sat Jul 25, 2009 6:07 pm 1 - In episode 16 during the Lelial mindfukc sequence, there was the dialouge that goes on about "the Ikari Shinji in so and so's mind". That whole scene made me think of how a person can appear to someone and yet still have a totally different personality. I know this doesn't sound like much of a revelation of the idea that there's a "you" that exists in someone else's mind was pretty radical the first time I heard it.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Jul 25, 2009 7:39 pm Reichu wrote: It's difficult to articulate Evangelion 's effect on me. I suppose the best way I can put it is that it took on the role of a narrative philosophical framework (I could probably use a better phrase) . Not so much in the sense that it provides principles to live by -- although it does offer some of those -- but it's a story about people, how we relate to one another and ourselves, and the daily struggle of living. It all resounded with me deeply, and I internalized it, and it became a part of me. So where some people might use, say, Bible stories as paradigms for episodes (etc.) in their own lives, Evangelion might be serving me instead.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sat Jul 25, 2009 8:09 pm Meh, and I just wrote that post to show just how rather complex philosophy in Evangelion can be - in fact it couldn't be much more complex... unless show would've headed into Wittgensteinian Philosophica Tractatus & philosophy of language field which gets so anal so quick even I have problems with reading this stuff for long despite my otherwise rather omnivoric nature with philosophy. And art *is* all about how you say and how you deal with your subject matters rather than subject matters per se. Buddha put it all down essentially in east and Ecclesiastes in west : "I have seen all the works that are done under the sun; and, behold, all is vanity and vexation of spirit." Funny thing is though I can't atm come up with any other work that attacks so enormously large scale of themes and philosophical musings as NGE does and manages to dramatize very complex ideas in superb manner, in fact in so superb manner I still haven't seen or read anything that equals it in this part. Instrumentality and Shinji's wanking scene can be (and should) be seen at least as triple metaphors and it is this multitude of themes show handles with essentially same events that's so outrageous and heavy about it. And *of course* I couldn't derive all that from Kaworu's single line, but given the whole context and subtext of show it is yet another one of those Anno lines that packs in quite incredible amount of thinking when you start to break it down as I hope I have shown. (and whether Anno did this consciously or not is completely irrelevant as work speaks for itself in this matter) Other factor is its honesty and intimacy. Anyone can go on quoting philosophical crap and entertain themselves with superficial philosophising; it is entirely different thing to pour your very essence into your work and wrestle to cure yourself through it, in continuous emotional and intellectual fight against yourself. That's what Anno did and that is very thing that separates NGE from so many other works such as Mamoru Oshii's. Sure, you can go on quoting and idly speculating about philosophy of mind but you ain't living what you are doing in the same way Anno did. And thus e.g GitS falls short where NGE doesn't. That's my take anyways.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Jul 25, 2009 9:14 pm Xard wrote: Meh, and I just wrote that post to show just how rather complex philosophy in Evangelion can be - in fact it couldn't be much more complex...I can't atm come up with any other work that attacks so enormously large scale of themes and philosophical musings as NGE does and manages to dramatize very complex ideas in superb manner, in fact in so superb manner I still haven't seen or read anything that equals it in this part... Other factor is its honesty and intimacy. Anyone can go on quoting philosophical crap and entertain themselves with superficial philosophising; it is entirely different thing to pour your very essence into your work and wrestle to cure yourself through it, in continuous emotional and intellectual fight against yourself.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Sun Jul 26, 2009 1:29 am for me, I would consider Evangelion to be life- affirming rather than life-changing way back in March of 2006 when I discovered Evangelion late one night on AS, I was going through a painful revelation of a situation my childhood friend had been going through, one which I recounted here on this forum as one of my first posts I had gone through other personal horror shows, some of which I recounted in the default blog I linked up in my sig for so long, I had these feelings on these situations in my life that everyone thought were somehow "inappropriate" or even "wrong", in that whenever I tried to talk someone, ANYONE, including my best friend and most beloved family members, they couldn't relate to these situations and at times they didn't give a damn, it was such a blow to my ego and self-esteem and for what seemed like an eternity it was like I was the only one who had these problems or at least was willing to admit such, I felt so alone, it was like it was an unrealistic fantasy of mine and everyone I was surrounded by to feel anything close to "empathy" but then I find Evangelion late one night while channel surfing, it was episode 26, and finally, I see all sorts of characters saying with great emotional weight the kinds of things that I had on my mind and tried to tell anyone I could for FCUKING YEARS there it was unfolding before me, I had such feelings of empathy and even wonder that I had not experienced since I was a child, along with some kind of fulfillment that I had found from a screwy, animated TV show made by a Japanese man no less I am and will always been in the emotional debt of Anno-sensei

[]:             Quote Post by Synapsid » Sun Jul 26, 2009 2:15 pm THE Hal E. Burton 9000 wrote: for me, I would consider Evangelion to be life- affirming rather than life-changing… for so long, I had these feelings on these situations in my life that everyone thought were somehow "inappropriate" or even "wrong", in that whenever I tried to talk someone, ANYONE, including my best friend and most beloved family members, they couldn't relate to these situations and at times they didn't give a damn, it was such a blow to my ego and self-esteem... I am and will always been in the emotional debt of Anno-sensei

But I can move around easier this way, so now I don't have to think about it, giving my mind freedom to think about other things. (I think that was the logic in episode 26 as well.) "True" freedom is like someone owning his own business. First, he has the freedom to do everything by himself. But that "true" freedom places restrictions on how well he performs those tasks. So he hires others to help do the work for him. Now he's restricted to only the tasks that he didn't delegate to others, but he has more freedom to perform his own tasks better. A car is also often seen as freedom, despite the fact that it restricts the movement of your legs, limits the functions of your hands, and forces you to pay attention to driving only. I don't think anyone really longs for true freedom. We enjoy some of our restrictions too much, and enjoyment is one of the freedoms we have.

I suppose the best way I can put it is that it took on the role of a narrative philosophical framework (I could probably use a better phrase) . Not so much in the sense that it provides principles to live by -- although it does offer some of those -- but it's a story about people, how we relate to one another and ourselves, and the daily struggle of living. It all resounded with me deeply, and I internalized it, and it became a part of me. So where some people might use, say, Bible stories as paradigms for episodes (etc.) in their own lives, Evangelion might be serving me instead.
